coredata.dc:title: A novel chiral fluorescence probe based on carbon dots-copper(II) system for ratio fluorescence detection of gatifloxacin
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Anli
Li, Renjie
Zhong, Yujia
Deng, Qunfen
Yin, Xinghang
Li, Haoyi
Kong, Ling
Yang, Rui
coredata.dc:description: Up to now, compared to the well-developed researches on chiral nanomaterials, there are relatively few studies focused on chiral Carbon Dots (CDs). The chiral fluorescent probes have properties of high sensitivity and good selectivity, which have attracted extensive attention. Herein, we constructed a chiral fluorescent probe based on a chiral CDs-Copper (II) system, having high photostability and sensitivity, which had the fast dual response of ratiometric fluorescence and enhanced circular dichroism (CD) to gatifloxacin (GAT). This method displayed a linear range of 0.01877–105.1 μg mL−1 with the detection limit of 0.04995 μg mL−1 for GAT under the optimized parameters. It has advantages of good selectivity, wide linear range, convenience and speediness. It was successfully applied to the detection of GAT in serum with satisfactory results. At the same time, the interaction of the chiral probes with chiral or achiral substrates indicates that the chiral CDs as new chiral functional materials have great application potential.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chiral fluorescence probe
Carbon dots
Gatifloxacin
Circular dichroism
Ratiometric fluorescent detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The fabrication of chiral fluorescent probes
Optical and chiroptical properties of L-/ D-Glu-CDs
The possible photoluminescence mechanism of L-/ D-Glu-CDs
The quenching mechanism of D-Glu-CDs by Cu2+
The chiral amplification of D-Glu-CDs@Cu2+ by GAT
Response of fluorescence spectra of D-Glu-CDs@Cu2+ to GAT
Interference and selectivity experiments of D-Glu-CDs@Cu2+-GAT
Detection of GAT in serum samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) and (B) TEM of L-/ D-Glu-CDs, the inset is HRTEM (upper right) and particle size distribution (bottom right) of L-/ D-Glu-CDs. (C) XRD pattern of L-Glu-CDs (blue line) and D-Glu-CDs (red line). (D) XPS survey spectra of L-Glu-CDs (blue line) and D-Glu-CDs (red line). Narrow scan spectra of (E) C1s, (F) N1s and (G) O1s, respectively.

Normalized spectra of UV–vis spectra and fluorescence spectra of L-Glu-CDs (A) and D-Glu-CDs (B), insets show the corresponding photos of L-/ D-Glu-CDs under daylight and a 365 nm UV lamp. Circular dichromatic spectra of L-/ D-Glu (C), and L-/ D-Glu-CDs (D).
Fluorescence intensity variation of L-/ D-Glu-CDs under the conditions of: (A) NaCl, (B) continuous illumination under ultraviolet lamp for 60 min, (C) pH (2–12) and (D) storage time.

(A) and (B) Fluorescence spectra of L-/ D-Glu-CDs, GAT, L-/ D-Glu-CDs@Cu2+ and L-/ D-Glu-CDs@Cu2+-GAT. Response of fluorescence spectra of D-Glu-CDs@Cu2+ to the addition of GAT ((C): 0–30 μg mL−1 and (D): 30.0–105.1 μg mL−1), the inset photos show the corresponding color evolutions under a 365 nm UV lamp. (E) The Plots of the absolute change of the F 494/F 418 of D-Glu-CDs@Cu2+-GAT versus the concentration of GAT (0–270.3 μg mL−1). (F) The linear relationship between the various concentration of GAT (0–105.1 μg mL−1) and F 494/F 418.

(A) The Tauc plots of the L-/ D-Glu-CDs. (B) VB XPS spectra of L-/ D-Glu-CDs. (C) Stern−Volmer plots of the D-Glu-CDs-Cu2+ system at different temperatures (283 K, 298 K and 323 K). (D) and (E) The CD spectra of L-/ D-Glu-CDs, L-/ D-Glu-CDs-Cu2+, and L-/ D-Glu-CDs@Cu2+-GAT, respectively. (F) The excitation (green) and emission (red) spectra of D-Glu-CDs@Cu2+, and the UV–vis spectra of Cu2+ (blue) and GAT (purple). (H) Schematic illustration of the possible PL emission mechanism for L-/ D-Glu-CDs.

(A) The response of quantitative D-Glu-CDs and GAT were obtained by using different media ions (Cu2+, Ag+, Mg2+, Ca2+, K+, Na+, Cd2+, Fe2+, Zn2+and Cr3+). (B) The selectivity of D-Glu-CDs@Cu2+ was quantitatively evaluated by PEF, NOR, CIP, LOM and GAT respectively. (C) and (D) The effects of different coexisting substances (H3BO4, K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Ag+, Co2+, Fe2+, Cr3+, I-, Cl-, NO3 -, SO4 2-, HPO4 -, Ala, DA, Urea, Gly, Pro, Sucrose, GLC, Adenine, NADP, Doxofylline, 4-Aminophenylacetic acid, Perfluorooctane Sulfonates (PFOS), Thymidine, H2O2, Inosine, Threonine and Guanidine thiocyanate) on D-Glu-CDs@Cu2+-GAT.
The synthesis process of L-/D-Glu-CDs (A), and the response of L-/D-Glu-CDs@Cu2+ to GAT in fluorescence spectra (B) and CD spectra (C), respectively.
Detection of GAT in human serum (n = 5).

head.ce:title: A novel chiral fluorescence probe based on carbon dots-copper(II) system for ratio fluorescence detection of gatifloxacin

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, No.2 Tiansheng Road, Chongqing 400715, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, No.2 Tiansheng Road, Chongqing 400715, People’s Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Up to now, compared to the well-developed researches on chiral nanomaterials, there are relatively few studies focused on chiral Carbon Dots (CDs). The chiral fluorescent probes have properties of high sensitivity and good selectivity, which have attracted extensive attention. Herein, we constructed a chiral fluorescent probe based on a chiral CDs-Copper (II) system, having high photostability and sensitivity, which had the fast dual response of ratiometric fluorescence and enhanced circular dichroism (CD) to gatifloxacin (GAT). This method displayed a linear range of 0.01877–105.1 μg mL−1 with the detection limit of 0.04995 μg mL−1 for GAT under the optimized parameters. It has advantages of good selectivity, wide linear range, convenience and speediness. It was successfully applied to the detection of GAT in serum with satisfactory results. At the same time, the interaction of the chiral probes with chiral or achiral substrates indicates that the chiral CDs as new chiral functional materials have great application potential.

ce:section.ce:para: Chiral materials play a paramount role in many fields such as recognition sensing [1], asymmetric catalysis [2], enantiodifferentiation [3] and biomedical applications [4]. Among them, chiral fluorescent probes not only have the advantages of high sensitivity and speediness of fluorescent sensors but also can achieve specific detection through the precise stereostructure [5]. As a novel fluorescent material, carbon dots (CDs) have been widely concerned and applied to fluorescent probes [6], biosensing [7], photocatalysis [8], bioimaging [9] and other aspects [10,11] due to their excellent optical properties, adjustable surface functions, favorable biocompatibility and excellent live-cell imaging performance. These studies have achieved many excellent results. While compared to extensive attention on achiral CDs, quite fewer studies on chiral CDs [12] have been carried out. Especially, they are seldom applied to ratiometric fluorescent detection [13]. Therefore, it is of great significance to further broaden the application of chiral CDs.

Gatifloxacin (GAT), one of the important fluoroquinolone agents (FAs), deserves particular concern among many antibiotics [14,15]. These popular drugs are used to treat bacterial infections in humans and added to livestock feed to treat animals [16]. However, the residues of GAT are released to the environment through the food chain cycle which can lead to the increase of microbial resistance [17] and the risk of the potential microbial outbreak [18]. At the same time, FAs metabolism is relatively slow in the body, which will lead to bioaccumulation and increasing toxicity in vivo, bringing a great threat to human health [19,20]. Therefore, GAT is also listed as an antibiotic contaminant in China [21], and its analysis and detection are of great significance in the field of environment and biology. Up to now, plenty of strategies have been currently developed for detecting GAT. Conventional techniques for GAT detection mainly include ultraviolet spectrophotometry [22], fluorescence method [23], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [24], electrochemical methods[25] and liquid chromatography-electrospray tandem mass spectrometry [26]. Among them, the fluorescence method has the advantages of simple operation, rapid response, low cost and high performance. Even though these methods have high accuracy, chiral fluorescent CDs with the dual response of ratiometric fluorescence and enhanced circular dichroism (CD) to GAT has never been reported.

In this contribution, we report the production of chiral CDs ((+) L-Glu-CDs and (+) D-Glu-CDs) by a one-step solvent-free method in the simultaneous presence of carbon source (citric acid monohydrate (CA)) and nitrogen source and chiral source (L-/D-glutamic acid (L- (+)-Glu or D-(-)-Glu). Then the morphology, structure, and optical properties of (+) L-Glu-CDs and (+) D-Glu-CDs (hereinafter called as L-Glu-CDs and D-Glu-CDs) were characterized. It is worth noting that the precursors used in the synthesis are enantiomorphs, however, L-Glu-CDs and D-Glu-CDs show similar chiral signals and different fluorescence properties. Although the interaction of Cu2+ with L-Glu-CDs or D-Glu-CDs would result in fluorescence quenching to a certain extent and maintain chirality, only D-Glu-CDs@Cu2+ system showed a double response of fluorescence and circular dichroism (CD) spectra for GAT detecting ( Scheme 1). Based on this, a novel chiral ratiometric fluorescent probe constructed by using D-Glu-CDs with Cu2+ for GAT detection based on förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) has been investigated. We optimized the experimental conditions for detection, studied the chiral origin of chiral carbon dots, and the possible reaction mechanism of the D-Glu-CDs@Cu2+ system to detect GAT. This method had the advantages of fast response, wide linear range, good stability and environmental friendliness. It had been applied to the determination of GAT in serum samples with satisfactory results.

1.0 g of citric acid monohydrate (CA) as carbon source and 2.1 g of L-(+)-Glu or D-(−)-Glu as nitrogen source were added into a beaker (50 mL) and mixed by using vortex mixers. Then the solution was transferred to the Teflon-lined stainless autoclave (50 mL), and placed into the sealed autoclave vessels of an electric oven at 180 ℃ for 4 h. After that, the product was cooled to room temperature, and dissolved with 5 mL of ultra-pure water by ultrasonic vibration for 10 min. The solution was then centrifuged at 8000 r min−1 for 10 min twice to remove insoluble large particles. The L-Glu-CDs raw solution was eluted with a large amount of acetone, and then the precipitation was dried at 70 ℃ to become L-Glu-CDs powder. The D-Glu-CDs stock solution was placed in a cellulose dialysis bag (MWCO: 500 Da) for 12 h and stored in the refrigerator. The CDs were finally collected by dissolving in water and freeze-drying, which were denoted as L-Glu-CDs and D-Glu-CDs, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The fabrication of chiral fluorescent probes
ce:section.ce:para: Suitable amounts of chiral carbon dots (L-/ D-Glu-CDs, 3.36 mg mL−1) and 0.1 mL of Cu2+ (5 mM) solution were added into 50 μL BR buffer solution (pH=6.5) and the resulting solution was diluted to 2.0 mL with double distilled water. Then it was vibrated and mixed. Eventually, chiral fluorescent probes L-/ D-Glu-CDs@Cu2+ were obtained.

8 mM GAT was prepared using ultrapure water as solvent as the stock solution. The working solutions with different concentrations were prepared by diluting the stock solution. The fluorescence probes of L-/ D-Glu-CDs@Cu2+ were added into 50 μL pH= 6.5 BR buffer solution respectively. Then, 0.1 mL of GAT working solution with different concentrations was added and diluted to the 2.0 mL calibration line with distilled water, which was oscillated and mixed uniformly at room temperature for 2 min. Finally, the fluorescence spectra, CD spectra, and UV–vis absorption spectra of the ternary system were measured at the excitation wavelength of 380 nm and 344 nm, respectively.

Morphological characteristics of the prepared L-Glu-CDs and D-Glu-CDs were obtained by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), indicating that CDs had a quasi-spherical structure and were well dispersivity. TEM images show that the chiral CDs have a rather centralized size distribution of approximately 3.5–8.0 nm and 1.5–5.0 nm ( Fig. 1A and B) and the average diameter of L-Glu-CDs and D-Glu-CDs were measured to be 4.5 nm or 3.4 nm respectively. And high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images (shown in the top right in Fig. 1A and B) of L-Glu-CDs and D-Glu-CDs further demonstrate atypical lattice spacing of 0.19 nm and 0.20 nm, which corresponded to the (100) in-plane lattice spacing of graphene [27–29]. The broad diffraction peaks of the L-Glu-CDs (blue line) and D-Glu-CDs (red line) observed in the XRD pattern (Fig. 1C) at around 23.5° and 21.2°, show that the crystal shape was not good enough [30–32].

FTIR characterization results were displayed in Fig. S5. The synthesized L-Glu-CDs and D-Glu-CDs showed absorption bands associated with O-H, N-H, and CC/CO stretching vibrations [33–36]. It displayed that they were composed of aromatic sp2 conjugated domain, surface oxygen and nitrogen-related groups [37]. Differences in internal structure and surface groups were further observed by XPS. Fig. 1D–G showed the XPS survey spectra of L-Glu-CDs (blue line) and D-Glu-CDs (red line) and the corresponding narrow scan spectra of C1s, N1s, and O1s, and the analytical results were listed in Table S1. The results indicated that the internal structure and surface groups of the two carbon dots are quite different. For example, the amounts of oxygen in the D-Glu-CDs were more than in the L-Glu-CDs, whereas the contents of nitrogen in D-Glu-CDs were insufficient. In addition, the content of graphite N and amino N on the surface of D-Glu-CDs were significantly inferior to that of L-Glu-CDs, while the content of pyridine N is significantly higher than that of L-Glu-CDs [34,38]. These differences in the internal structure and surface groups of the carbon nucleus may be responsible for the different fluorescence spectra and circular dichroic properties of L-/ D-Glu-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical and chiroptical properties of L-/ D-Glu-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 A and B showed the absorption and fluorescence spectra of L-Glu-CDs and D-Glu-CDs. They all had one broad peak at 348 and 310 nm in the UV–vis absorption spectra, which were assigned to the n-π * transition of C-N and CO [13,39,40]. When the excitation wavelength changed from 270 to 450 nm, as shown in Figs. S6 and S7, the positions of the corresponding PL peak all shifted, showing excitation-dependent PL behavior [41]. The bright and colorful PL might be attributed to the synergistic effect of the carbogenic core and the surface state of the L-/ D-Glu-CDs [42]. L-/ D-Glu-CDs achieved the maximum emission intensity around 454 nm and 418 nm when they were excited at 380 nm and 344 nm, which were all typical characteristics of blue fluorescent carbon dots [43]. The quantum yields of the two L-/ D-Glu-CDs were also measured to be about 87.7% and 32.0%. The subsequent experiments were performed at the optimal excitation wavelengths of the L-/ D-Glu-CDs. The CD spectra of chiral precursors and their products were shown in Fig. 2C and D. It could be seen that although the precursors were chiral enantiomers, their products showed the same Cotton signal preference. These results indicated that the carbon dots prepared by L-(+)-Glu and D-(−)-Glu under the same synthetic conditions showed chiral retention and chiral inversion of the chiral source, respectively, possibly due to the reaction temperature [44].

The stability of L-Glu-CDs or D-Glu-CDs was studied under different conditions. According to previous reports, we chose a higher concentration of salt ions to determine that they all have great salt tolerance [45]. And when L-Glu-CDs or D-Glu-CDs (3.36 mg mL−1) were added into different concentrations of NaCl (0–2.5 mol L−1), their fluorescence intensity was kept unchanged basically ( Fig. 3A). The results indicated that L-/ D-Glu-CDs had better ionic strength stability and could be used in more complex samples. After continuous illumination under an ultraviolet lamp for a period (0–60 min), their fluorescence intensity also remained almost unchanged (Fig. 3B), indicating that they had great optical stability. In addition, the CDs still had high stability at different pH values (2−12) (Fig. 3C) and after long-term storage (Fig. 3D).

The fluorescence responses of chiral probes of L-/ D-Glu-CDs@Cu2+ with GAT were shown in Fig. 4A and B, which displayed the fluorescence of L-/ D-Glu-CDs were partially quenched by Cu2+. When GAT was added separately to L-/ D-Glu-CDs@Cu2+, the fluorescence intensity of L-Glu-CDs@Cu2+ had no change, however, the fluorescence spectrum of D-Glu-CDs@Cu2+ changed significantly. Thus, the fluorescence probe of D-Glu-CDs@Cu2+ could be constructed to detect GAT selectively.

Based on this, the influence of the D-Glu-CDs@Cu2+ probe to detect GAT under different conditions, such as pH, time, the concentration of Cu2+ and reaction temperature were investigated, and the results were shown in Figs. S1–S4, respectively. Under the optimal experimental parameters, the fluorescence intensity of D-Glu-CDs@Cu2+ at different GAT concentrations was monitored. Fig. 4C shows the opposite changes of F 418 and F 494 with the concentration of GAT increased from 0 to 30.0 μg mL−1. Obviously, with the addition of GAT, the emission peak of D-Glu-CDs@Cu2+ at 418 nm decreased sequentially, and a new emission peak appeared at 494 nm, which caused an increase in the ratio fluorescence intensity of F 494/F 418. Notably, as the concentration of GAT increased from 30.0 to 105.1 μg mL−1, the fluorescence intensity at both 418 nm and 494 nm decreased (Fig. 4D), however, the ratios of F 494/F 418 still rose gradually (Fig. 4E). The ratios of F 494/F 418 kept increasing at GAT concentrations ranging from 0 to 105.1 μg mL−1 and then reached a plateau with continued increased content of GAT.

Furthermore, the linear fitting equation was F 494/F 418 = 0.1571 CGAT + 0.7811, (R² = 0.9993) (Fig. 4F), the linear range were 0.01877–105.1 μg mL−1, and the detection limit was calculated to be 0.04995 μg mL−1. The other LODs for GAT by extraction methods in the reported literatures were provided in Table S2. As seen from the results, the LOD of this present work was significantly lower.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The possible photoluminescence mechanism of L-/ D-Glu-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In the synthetic process of the chiral CDs, citric acid, a tricarboxylic compound that easily forms a ring, was used as a C source, and L-/ D-glutamate was used as an N source and chiral source with structural and functional units. Dehydration and condensation of amino and carboxyl groups led to C-N/CN cross-linking and gradually form of the carbogenic core. Whereafter, many N- and O-related surface functional groups of amino acids were retained and caused structural defects at the edges of graphene fragments, resulting in multiple energy level (nO, nN) transitions to produce the light absorption. We suggested that the fluorescence emission of L-/D-Glu-CDs was due to the synergistic effect of carbogenic core and surface defects. The band edge (Eg) of L-/ D-Glu-CDs had been calculated through Tauc curves (S 2.4). As shown in Fig. 5A, it revealed that 5.98 eV and 5.73 eV were the Eg values of L-/ D-Glu-CDs, respectively. The density of states of the valence band (VB) of CDs were measured by the VB XPS, showing the VB energy levels of 2.68 eV and 2.76 eV for L-/ D-Glu-CDs, (Fig. 5B). Based on this, the conduction band (CB) energy levels of L-/ D-Glu-CDs were calculated to be −3.30 and −2.97 eV, respectively. Herein, schematic diagrams of illustration of energy level and electronic transition of L-/ D-Glu-CDs had been proposed as seen in Fig. 5H. Due to the introduction of oxygen and nitrogen elements, there existed multiple emissive traps and different emission centers in the surface defect states located between the conduction band and the valence band, leading to the wavelength-dependent emission of L-/ D-Glu-CDs [41,46]. Moreover, the FL lifetime decay curves of D-Glu-CDs confirmed double exponential fittings (Fig. S8 and Table S3), implying that two luminescence centers existed in the CDs [47].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The quenching mechanism of D-Glu-CDs by Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: It was found that the Stern-Volmer constant (K SV) of Cu2+ (S 2.5) to D-Glu-CDs at three temperatures (283 K, 298 K and 323 K) decreased with the increase in temperature (Fig. 5C). In addition, the fluorescence lifetime of D-Glu-CDs was almost unchanged (τ0 / τ = 1.06) before and after Cu2+ addition (Fig. S8 and Table S3). The above-mentioned analysis implied the mechanism was static quenching (SQE) [48].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The chiral amplification of D-Glu-CDs@Cu2+ by GAT
ce:section.ce:para: The CD spectra of L-/ D-Glu-CDs, L-/ D-Glu-CDs@Cu2+ and L-/ D-Glu-CDs@Cu2+-GAT were shown in Fig. 5D and E, and the details of changes of CD spectra were discussed in S4. The dramatic shift of the intersection of the CD curve and the 0 scale line from 233 nm to 209 nm, and the increased dissymmetry factor (G factor) (S 2.6) from 2.20 to 4.07, indicated that the chirality of D-Glu-CDs@Cu2+ was amplified [49] after the addition of GAT. Interestingly, the CD signals of L-Glu-CDs@Cu2+ and D-Glu-CDs@Cu2+ were positive Cotton and had completely different responses to GAT, implying that the chirality sources of the L-/ D-Glu-CDs were different. Chiral CDs could be produced from the chiral precursors via easy, usually one-step synthesis schemes and mild conditions (such as lower reaction temperatures) [50]. Thence, chiral CDs would retain the “structural memory” of the building blocks [50,51]. Combined with the experimental results of the previous XPS and FTIR, it could be speculated that the surface amino nitrogen of L-Glu-CDs retained more spatial structures of the chiral source. Therefore, the CD signal of L-Glu-CDs was consistent with that of L-Glu. Whereas D-Glu-CDs formed concomitantly more pyridine N and a rigid structure, and its chirality may be more derived from the steric effect of the carbon nucleus [52] of CDs, resulting in chirality inversion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response of fluorescence spectra of D-Glu-CDs@Cu2+ to GAT
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 4C, with rising GAT concentrations, the fluorescence intensity of D-Glu-CDs@Cu2+ at 418 nm was quenched sequentially, and at the same time a new emission peak at 494 nm increased. As seen in Fig. 5F, there was a partial overlap between the absorption spectrum of GAT (quencher) at 200–400 nm and the excitation spectrum of D-Glu-CDs@Cu2+, suggesting that the presence of GAT might lead to the fluorescence quenching of D-Glu-CDs@Cu2+ by Internal filtering effect (IFE) or Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). Table S3 showed that the fluorescence lifetime of D-Glu-CDs@Cu2+-GAT (4.69 ns) was shorter than that of D-Glu-CDs@Cu2+ (15.68 ns), indicating that the quenching mechanism was FRET [53], not IFE [45,54]. At the same time, changes in the UV-Vis absorption spectra (Fig. S9) suggested that a complex, D-Glu-CDs@Cu2+-GAT, was formed by GAT combining with D-Glu-CDs@Cu2+, resulting in a new emission peak at 494 nm. The clear isoemission point at 440 nm (Fig. 4C) also confirmed the formation of this new complex.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interference and selectivity experiments of D-Glu-CDs@Cu2+-GAT
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, we constructed the probes of D-Glu-CDs@Mn+ by using common metal ions for detecting GAT, the results were shown in Fig. 6A. It could be seen that, compared to other metal ions, Cu2+ showed extremely high sensitivity and selectivity. This might be attributed to the fact that the fluorescence of D-Glu-CDs could be effectively quenched by Cu2+ (Fig. S10). Consequently, D-Glu-CDs@Cu2+ was constructed as a fluorescent probe to detect GAT in this work. Next, we investigated the response of the D-Glu-CDs@Cu2+ probe to GAT analogs (such as pefloxacin (PEF), norfloxacin (NOR), ciprofloxacin (CIP), and lomefloxacin (LOM)), shown in Fig. 6B. The responses of GAT analogs were much lower than that of GAT and cannot show the fluorescence response of dual-emissive (Fig. S11). It proved that the chiral fluorescent probe of D-Glu-CDs@Cu2+ could detect GAT with high selectivity. To evaluate the potential interferences of the designed probe for detecting GAT, the fluorescence response of the D-Glu-CDs@Cu2+-GAT system toward various coexisting materials (the concentration of H3BO4, K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Ag+, Co2+, Cr3+, I-, Cl-, NO3 -, SO4 2-, HPO4 -, DA, Urea, Pro, Sucrose, GLC, Adenine, NADP, Doxofylline, 4-Aminophenylacetic acid, Perfluorooctane Sulfonates (PFOS), Thymidine, H2O2, Inosine, Threonine, and Guanidine thiocyanate were 10 times that of GAT, and Fe3+, Ala and Gly were 5 times that of the concentration of GAT.) was investigated (Fig. 6C and D). Most of the coexisting substances did not cause significant interference to the detection of GAT in the D-Glu-CDs@Cu2+ system, which indicated that the system had a strong anti-interference ability and was expected to be used for GAT analysis in practical samples with complex composition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of GAT in serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: To examine the possibility of this method for the detection of GAT in real samples, a human serum sample, was quantified by this system. Fasting human serum samples provided by young healthy volunteers were selected and centrifuged at 8000 r min−1 for 5 min, then the supernatant was diluted 100 times, and GAT was determined by the standard addition method. The different concentrations of GAT (30, 45, 60, 75 and 90 μg mL−1) were spiked into human serum samples, which were used to mimic real samples. According to the results shown in Table 1, high recovery rates ranging from 96.5% to 106.9% were achieved with RSDs (n = 5) of 0.9–2.7%, and the found concentrations of GAT were in good agreement with the spiked concentration. No GAT was found in unspiked serum samples using the proposed method. The results successfully confirmed the applicability of the proposed chiral probe for the measurement of GAT in real samples.

In short, the chiral carbon dots were successfully synthesized by using L-(+)-Glu and D-(−)-Glu as the chiral and nitrogen sources and condensed with citric acid by a simple one-step solvent-free process. The results of morphology and spectral characterization showed that the chirality of L-Glu-CDs was derived from the stereoscopic structure of its chiral precursor, while D-Glu-CDs might be derived from the spatial effect of the carbon core. In contrast, a series of experiments revealed that, compared to those probes constructed by chiral CDs with other common ions, D-Glu-CDs@Cu2+ were especially outstanding for detection of GAT due to their stereoselectivity. To the best of present knowledge, this was the first chiral fluorescent probe with high selectivity and high sensitivity for GAT detection based on the dual response of fluorescence and CD spectra. This research strategy provided a new mentality for the application of chiral carbon dots and showed great potential in chiral recognition and drug analysis.
All persons who have made substantial contributions to the work reported in the manuscript are named. All authors have seen and approved the final version of the manuscript being submitted.

We warrant that the article is the authors' original work, hasn't received prior publication and isn't under consideration for publication elsewhere. We have drafted the work or revised it critically for important intellectual content. We agree to be accountable for all aspects of the work in ensuring that questions related to the accuracy or integrity of any part of the work are appropriately investigated and resolved.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors. Thanks to eceshi (www.eceshi.cn) for the help of XPS and TEM analysis. Special thanks to the group of Professor Jiang Yunbao of Xiamen University for their support of CD spectroscopy analysis.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.131602.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Chiral CuxOS@ZIF‐8 nanostructures for ultrasensitive quantification of hydrogen sulfide in vivo

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Yuan, C. Hao, X. Wu, M. Sun, A. Qu, L. Xu, H. Kuang, C. Xu, Chiral CuxOS@ZIF‐8 Nanostructures for Ultrasensitive Quantification of Hydrogen Sulfide In Vivo, Adv. Mater. 32 (2020) 1906580.
